was the 74th annual meeting of [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) franchises to select newly eligible football players. The [draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") took place at [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), New York, on April 25 and 26, [2009](/wiki/2009_NFL_season "2009 NFL season").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The draft consisted of two rounds on the first day, starting at 4:00 pm [EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"), and five rounds on the second day, starting at 10:00 am EDT. To compensate for the time change from the previous year and in an effort to help shorten the draft, teams were no longer on the clock for 15 minutes in the first round and 10 minutes in the second round. Each team now had 10 minutes to make their selection in the first round and seven minutes in the second round. Rounds three through seven were shortened to five minutes per team. This was the first year that the NFL used this format and it was changed again the following year for the 2010 NFL draft. The 2009 NFL draft was televised by both [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") and was the first to have cheerleaders. The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions"), who became the first team in NFL history to finish [a season at 0–16](/wiki/2008_Detroit_Lions_season "2008 Detroit Lions season"), used the first selection in the draft to select [University of Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_Bulldogs_football "Georgia Bulldogs football") [quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford").[[3]](#cite_note-stafford-3)

The **2009 NFL draft** was the 74th annual meeting of [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) franchises to select newly eligible football players. The [draft](/wiki/NFL_draft "NFL draft") took place at [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), New York, on April 25 and 26, [2009](/wiki/2009_NFL_season "2009 NFL season").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The draft consisted of two rounds on the first day, starting at 4:00 pm [EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone"), and five rounds on the second day, starting at 10:00 am EDT. To compensate for the time change from the previous year and in an effort to help shorten the draft, teams were no longer on the clock for 15 minutes in the first round and 10 minutes in the second round. Each team now had 10 minutes to make their selection in the first round and seven minutes in the second round. Rounds three through seven were shortened to five minutes per team. This was the first year that the NFL used this format and it was changed again the following year for the 2010 NFL draft. The 2009 NFL draft was televised by both [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") and was the first to have cheerleaders. The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions"), who became the first team in NFL history to finish [a season at 0–16](/wiki/2008_Detroit_Lions_season "2008 Detroit Lions season"), used the first selection in the draft to select [University of Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_Bulldogs_football "Georgia Bulldogs football") [quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford").[[3]](#cite_note-stafford-3)

It was the first draft since [1983](/wiki/1983_NFL_draft "1983 NFL draft") that saw two [centers](/wiki/Center_(American_football) "Center (American football)") being selected in the first round—[Alex Mack](/wiki/Alex_Mack "Alex Mack") at No. 21 to the Browns, and [Eric Wood](/wiki/Eric_Wood "Eric Wood") at No. 28 to the Bills. It was also the first time since the [1993](/wiki/1993_NFL_draft "1993 NFL draft") draft that a [Miami Hurricanes](/wiki/Miami_Hurricanes_football "Miami Hurricanes football") player was not selected in the first round. As of the end of the 2018 season, the 2009 draft has seen 11 of the 32 first-round selections make the Pro Bowl, and 27 (including three punters) in total for the entire class.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It has been referred to as one of the worst drafts in league history.[[5]](#cite_note-5) This was the first time that a Mr. Irrelevant went on to win a Super Bowl ([Ryan Succop](/wiki/Ryan_Succop "Ryan Succop")).[[6]](#cite_note-6)

As of 2025, the only remaining active players in the NFL from the 2009 draft class are Rams quarterback [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford"), 49ers punter [Thomas Morstead](/wiki/Thomas_Morstead "Thomas Morstead"), and Giants placekicker [Graham Gano](/wiki/Graham_Gano "Graham Gano").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Matt_Stafford_Chase_Young_%2850611111796%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Matt_Stafford_Chase_Young_%2850611111796%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matt_Stafford_Chase_Young_(50611111796)_(cropped).jpg)

First overall pick and the 2025 [NFL Most Valuable Player](/wiki/Associated_Press_NFL_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "Associated Press NFL Most Valuable Player Award") [Matthew Stafford](/wiki/Matthew_Stafford "Matthew Stafford") is the fastest quarterback to reach 40,000 passing yards in NFL history and led the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams") to a victory in [Super Bowl LVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI "Super Bowl LVI").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Brian_Orakpo1.jpg/250px-Brian_Orakpo1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brian_Orakpo1.jpg)

Linebacker [Brian Orakpo](/wiki/Brian_Orakpo "Brian Orakpo"), taken 13th overall, was a 4-time [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") selection.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Malcolm_Jenkins.JPG/250px-Malcolm_Jenkins.JPG)](/wiki/File:Malcolm_Jenkins.JPG)

Cornerback [Malcolm Jenkins](/wiki/Malcolm_Jenkins "Malcolm Jenkins"), drafted 14th overall, won two Super Bowls with two different teams and was named to 3 Pro Bowls.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Clay_Matthews_-_San_Francisco_vs_Green_Bay_2012.jpg/250px-Clay_Matthews_-_San_Francisco_vs_Green_Bay_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clay_Matthews_-_San_Francisco_vs_Green_Bay_2012.jpg)

Linebacker [Clay Matthews III](/wiki/Clay_Matthews_III "Clay Matthews III"), picked 26th overall, is a member of the [Matthews family](/wiki/Matthews_family "Matthews family") of football players and was one of the league's premier pass-rushers throughout his career.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Alex_Mack.jpg/250px-Alex_Mack.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alex_Mack.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Eric_Wood_2016.jpg/250px-Eric_Wood_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eric_Wood_2016.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Max_Unger.jpg/250px-Max_Unger.jpg)](/wiki/File:Max_Unger.jpg)

Prominent centers taken in this draft (from top to bottom): [Alex Mack](/wiki/Alex_Mack "Alex Mack"), [Eric Wood](/wiki/Eric_Wood "Eric Wood"), and [Max Unger](/wiki/Max_Unger "Max Unger").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Lesean_mccoy_2014.jpg/250px-Lesean_mccoy_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lesean_mccoy_2014.jpg)

Running back [LeSean McCoy](/wiki/LeSean_McCoy "LeSean McCoy"), drafted in the second round, is the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles")' leader in career rushing yards, a two-time [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro"), a six-time [Pro Bowler](/wiki/Pro_Bowler "Pro Bowler") and a two-time Super Bowl champion.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/CookJared2013-8.jpg/250px-CookJared2013-8.jpg)](/wiki/File:CookJared2013-8.jpg)

Tight end [Jared Cook](/wiki/Jared_Cook "Jared Cook"), drafted in the third round, is a two-time [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") selection.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Pat_McAfee.JPG/250px-Pat_McAfee.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pat_McAfee.JPG)

Punter [Pat McAfee](/wiki/Pat_McAfee "Pat McAfee"), selected in the seventh round, was a two-time [Pro Bowl](/wiki/Pro_Bowl "Pro Bowl") selection and [All-Pro](/wiki/All-Pro "All-Pro") for the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Julian_Edelman_2014.JPG/250px-Julian_Edelman_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:Julian_Edelman_2014.JPG)

Slot receiver [Julian Edelman](/wiki/Julian_Edelman "Julian Edelman"), drafted in the seventh round, won three Super Bowls with the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots"), and was selected as [MVP](/wiki/Super_Bowl_MVP "Super Bowl MVP") of [Super Bowl LIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIII "Super Bowl LIII").

## Trades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_draft&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Trades")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Commissioner_Goodell_2009_NFL_Draft.jpg/250px-Commissioner_Goodell_2009_NFL_Draft.jpg)](/wiki/File:Commissioner_Goodell_2009_NFL_Draft.jpg)

NFL commissioner Roger Goodell announcing a pick at the 2009 draft.

In the explanations below, **(D)** denotes trades that took place during the draft, while **(PD)** indicates trades completed pre-draft.

**Round 1**

1. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** **No. 5: Cleveland → NY Jets (D).** Cleveland traded a first-round selection (5th overall) to the NY Jets in exchange for first- and second-round selections (17th and 52nd overall), DE [Kenyon Coleman](/wiki/Kenyon_Coleman "Kenyon Coleman"), QB [Brett Ratliff](/wiki/Brett_Ratliff "Brett Ratliff") and S [Abram Elam](/wiki/Abram_Elam "Abram Elam").[[source 1]](#cite_note-14)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-17)** **No. 17: NY Jets → Cleveland → Tampa Bay.** Multiple trades:  
          **NY Jets → Cleveland (D).** See Round 1: [Cleveland → NY Jets](#Sanchez).  
          **Cleveland → Tampa Bay (D).** Cleveland traded a first-round selection (17th overall) to Tampa Bay in exchange for first- and sixth-round selections (19th and 191st overall).[[source 2]](#cite_note-16)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** **No. 18: Chicago → Denver (PD).** Chicago traded first- and third-round selections (18th and 84th overall), a 2010 first-round selection and QB [Kyle Orton](/wiki/Kyle_Orton "Kyle Orton") to Denver in exchange for a fifth-round selection (140th overall) and QB [Jay Cutler](/wiki/Jay_Cutler "Jay Cutler").[[source 3]](#cite_note-18)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** **No. 19: Tampa Bay → Cleveland → Philadelphia.** Multiple trades:  
          **Tampa Bay → Cleveland (D).** See Round 1: [Cleveland → Tampa Bay](#TampaTradeUp).  
          **Cleveland → Philadelphia (D).** Cleveland traded a first-round selection (19th overall) to Philadelphia in exchange for first- and sixth-round selections (21st and 195th overall).[[source 4]](#cite_note-20)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** **No. 20: Dallas → Detroit (PD).** Dallas traded first-, third- and sixth-round selections (20th, 82nd and 192nd overall) to Detroit in exchange for a seventh-round selection (210th overall) and WR [Roy Williams](/wiki/Roy_Williams_(wide_receiver) "Roy Williams (wide receiver)").[[source 5]](#cite_note-22)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** **No. 21: Philadelphia → Cleveland (D).** See Round 1: [Cleveland → Philadelphia](#PhiladelphiaTradeUp).
7. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** **No. 23: New England → Baltimore (D).** New England traded a first-round selection (23rd overall) to Baltimore in exchange for first- and fifth-round selections (26th and 162nd overall).[[source 6]](#cite_note-25)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** **No. 26: Baltimore → New England → Green Bay.** Multiple trades:  
          **Baltimore → New England (D).** See Round 1: [New England → Baltimore](#BaltimoreTradeUp).  
          **New England → Green Bay (D).** New England traded first- and fifth-round selections (26th and 162nd overall) to Green Bay in exchange for a second-round selection and two third-round selections (41st, 73rd and 83rd overall).[[source 7]](#cite_note-27)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** **No. 28: Carolina → Philadelphia → Buffalo.** Multiple trades:  
          **Carolina → Philadelphia (PD).** Carolina traded a first-round selection (28th overall), and 2008 second- and fourth-round selections to Philadelphia in exchange for a 2008 first-round selection.[[source 8]](#cite_note-29)  
          **Philadelphia → Buffalo (PD).** Philadelphia traded first- and fourth-round selections (28th and 121st overall) to Buffalo in exchange for OT [Jason Peters](/wiki/Jason_Peters "Jason Peters").[[source 9]](#cite_note-30)
